scale.intro: How easy is the text to read on the layout: is the headline visible, does text blend into the background, are there too many different fonts? If you are not a designer, ask yourself: “Can I read the main message in a couple of seconds?” This page explains scores from 0 to 5 for each criterion. The higher the score, the better the layout.

sources.title: J. Felici, Typography: Type, Layout, and Design
R. Bringhurst, The Elements of Typographic Style
T. Stone et al., Color Design Workbook
T. Makarova, N. Samsonova, The Role of Color in Advertising Imagery
J. Müller-Brockmann, Grid Systems in Graphic Design
S. Krug, Don't Make Me Think

levels.title: Absent
levels.description: No text, or labels are random letters and decoration with no meaning. Where words are needed, they are missing.

levels.title: Critical
levels.description: Text is physically unreadable: it blends into the background, is too small, or uses three or more fonts with no system.

levels.title: Poor
levels.description: Readable, but not at a glance: the headline barely differs from body text, lines are too tight or too loose, fonts clash.

levels.title: Fair
levels.description: Text is readable if you look closely. Headline and body are distinguishable, but polish is missing — some spots are too small or low-contrast.

levels.title: Good
levels.description: Text reads at a glance. The headline is clearly larger, one or two fonts are used, letters do not get lost on the background. Everything looks tidy and clear.

levels.title: Excellent
levels.description: At first glance it is obvious what matters most. Fonts work together, text is easy to read on screen or in print, typography supports the message instead of fighting it.

levels.title: Absent
levels.description: Color does nothing for the task: the whole layout is one dull or random tone with no accents. Colors are not tied to the brand and do not show what matters.

levels.title: Critical
levels.description: Overload: many bright, clashing colors at once — every block shouts on its own. The eye gets tired; it is unclear what to look at first.

levels.title: Poor
levels.description: Few colors, but they do not help: the image is gray and dull, or two–three shades clash. There is no clear accent color for a button, headline, or main message.

levels.title: Fair
levels.description: Not too many colors; acceptable to look at. There is some accent, but its role is not always obvious — the most important part is not instantly clear.

levels.title: Good
levels.description: A calm, clear palette: background, text, and accent color work together. Brightness is moderate; colors help highlight what matters.

levels.title: Excellent
levels.description: Colors work as a team: each has a role, combinations are pleasing, accents guide attention without overload. The palette supports the brand mood and message.

levels.title: Absent
levels.description: Everything looks equally important: headline, images, and small text share the same size and weight. No hint where to start looking.

levels.title: Critical
levels.description: There should be a main message, but it is invisible: the headline is lost among images and decoration; all blocks are equally loud.

levels.title: Poor
levels.description: A main element exists, but secondary parts overpower it: bright images nearby, too many small details, little free space around the key block.

levels.title: Fair
levels.description: The main element can be found, but you need a moment to search. Secondary details are still too loud — reading order is not obvious.

levels.title: Good
levels.description: The main thing is clear at once: headline or key image stands out by size, color, or contrast. The eye then moves naturally to details.

levels.title: Excellent
levels.description: It is instantly clear what matters most and what you can skip. The button or key block is visible but not annoying. The eye moves calmly from the main point to details — no confusion or extra effort.

levels.title: Absent
levels.description: Elements are scattered without logic: no alignment, everything floats, the layout looks like a random pile of images and text.

levels.title: Critical
levels.description: The layout breaks: blocks overlap, content is pressed to the edges with no margins, one half of the screen is empty while the other is packed tight.

levels.title: Poor
levels.description: No overlaps, but the layout is crooked: more material on one side, elements not aligned, margins either huge or almost zero — everything looks uneven.

levels.title: Fair
levels.description: Mostly even, but without character. Basic alignment exists, yet the layout feels template-like — no strong visual center.

levels.title: Good
levels.description: Balanced layout: elements sit in the right places, margins at the edges, main blocks placed logically. The image looks assembled.

levels.title: Excellent
levels.description: Composition guides the eye and looks professional: balance is intentional, proportions fit, elements follow a clear structure — as if drawn with a ruler.
